questions.question: Explain the difference between onClick and onTouch event handling in Android. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: onClick handles only tap completion; onTouch handles entire touch sequence including DOWN, MOVE, UP
onTouch provides MotionEvent with coordinates, pressure, and action codes
Use onClick for simple button clicks; use onTouch for drag, swipe, custom gestures
onTouch returns boolean to consume events; onClick is void return type

questions.question: Describe the three methods to implement click event handling in Android with code snippets.
keyPoints: XML android:onClick attribute - method reference in layout
Anonymous inner class - setOnClickListener(new...)
Activity implements OnClickListener - class-level implementation
Each has trade-offs: XML loses compile-time checking; Anonymous creates objects; Activity implements couples UI and logic

questions.question: What is event propagation in Android? Explain the capture and bubbling phases with an example.
keyPoints: Event propagation has three phases: Capture (root to target), Target (at view), Bubbling (target to root)
Android defaults to bubbling phase
Parent view can intercept events in onInterceptTouchEvent()
Example: Touch on nested button bubbles up if child doesn't consume it

questions.question: How does GestureDetector simplify touch event handling? List two common gestures it can detect.
keyPoints: GestureDetector wraps raw MotionEvents into meaningful gesture callbacks
SimpleOnGestureListener provides default implementations
Detects: onFling (swipe), onScroll (drag), onDoubleTap, onLongPress, onSingleTapConfirmed, onDoubleTapEvent
Reduces code complexity compared to manual touch event handling

questions.question: Write the code to implement a button that detects both single click and long press, with long press showing a Toast and single click displaying a different message.
keyPoints: Set OnClickListener for single tap - show Toast('Single Click')
Set OnLongClickListener for long press - show Toast('Long Press')
Return true from onLongClick to consume event
Alternative: Return false to allow both handlers to fire

questions.question: Create a custom view that implements a drag-and-drop functionality. Explain the touch event handling logic required to detect drag start, drag move, and drag end.
keyPoints: Override onTouchEvent or set OnTouchListener
ACTION_DOWN: Save initial coordinates, flag drag started
ACTION_MOVE: Calculate delta, update view position, invalidate
ACTION_UP: Complete drag, reset flags, handle drop target
Use getRawX() and getRawY() for absolute positioning
Consider View.OnDragListener for drop targets

questions.question: Design an Android activity that implements a drawing app. The app should support drawing lines with finger movement. Explain the event handling architecture including multi-touch support.
keyPoints: Use OnTouchListener on custom Canvas View
Track multiple pointers using MotionEvent.getPointerCount() and getPointerId()
On ACTION_DOWN: Start new path, move to touch point
On ACTION_MOVE: For each pointer, lineTo() new coordinates, invalidate view
On ACTION_UP/CANCEL: End current path, save state
Store Path objects in ArrayList with Paint settings
Use separate Path for each stroke to allow individual manipulation

questions.question: What is the purpose of requestDisallowInterceptTouchEvent()? In what scenario would you use it?
keyPoints: Prevents parent ViewGroup from intercepting touch events
Used in nested scrolling scenarios
Example: ViewPager inside ScrollView - child calls this to prevent parent from stealing horizontal swipes
Called on parent view from child view to signal 'don't intercept during my gesture'
Part of ViewGroup's requestDisallowInterceptTouchEvent(boolean) method
